Patch Set 2:

(1 comment)

Patch Set 2:

the reason to nest locking inside transaction is to improve concurrency. so that we don't need to  lock a whole object for a whole transaction. I think we should do the same in OFD.

Patch Set 2:

> the reason to nest locking inside transaction is to improve concurrency. so that we don't need to  lock a whole object for a whole transaction. I think we should do the same in OFD.

Not quite understand. Currently, on the MDT side, dt_lock is after the transaction started and dt_unlock before the transaction stop, but there may be transaction restart (by ldiskfs) with dt_lock held. So what is your expected behavior? when dt_lock and when dt_unlock?

Patch Set 2:

the only case with a transaction restart is truncate, I think we should consider that as an exception. taking the locks with a transaction held improves scalability in some cases (like attribute updates). so I'd say we shold try to follow this model on OFD too.

Patch Set 2:

> the only case with a transaction restart is truncate, I think we should consider that as an exception. taking the locks with a transaction held improves scalability in some cases (like attribute updates). so I'd say we shold try to follow this model on OFD too.

From the implementation view, how can we handle truncate as a special case? If all other modifications for OST-object will take lock after transaction started, that is the same as MDD side does, then the truncate on the OFD has to follow the same rule; otherwise, truncate and other modification on the same OST-object may cause deadlock. But if we make the truncate to take lock after transaction started, then once transaction restart during the truncate, then it also may cause deadlock with other modification.

Patch Set 2:

PPC64 Builder: Cannot build patch due to 10, messages:

 git checkout error!

Patch Set 2:

Alex, could you please respond to Fan Yong's comments.

Patch Set 2:

notice DoM will hit the same issue, soe we better solve the original problem.. need to think.

Patch Set 2:

a small update.. first of all, it seems ZFS doesn't need to restart transaction. so we need a solution for ldiskfs only. then the only competitors for truncate are another trancate and write. i.e. we need to serialize truncate vs truncate/write and this must be done before transaction starts. I _think_ it should be possible to grab an exclusive/shared separate lock (inside OSD) for truncate and write at declaration and release it in osd_trans_stop(). it's pretty tricky as we'll have to ensure not to hold any locks (except the suggested one) starting truncate. yet another (elegant  ;) option is to rework ldiskfs's truncate and use some kind of blocks migration so that block mapping/extent tree is updated to reflect new state, but actual release of blocks is postponed. I'm sure this is doable, but a bit more work.

also, I doubt that we can change transaction/lock ordering as doing locks on per-update basis (in contrast with per-operation) is vital for MDT scalability.

Patch Set 2:

Back in the old days we had the unlink/truncate thread to run in the background, which would be an option. However, any significant change to ldiskfs would be needed for all kernel versions we support forever, so it probably makes more sense to do the fixing in osd-ldiskfs instead of in ldiskfs itself.

Patch Set 2:

>  I _think_ it should be possible to grab an exclusive/shared separate lock (inside OSD) for truncate and write at declaration and release it in osd_trans_stop(). it's pretty tricky as we'll have to ensure not to hold any locks (except the suggested one) starting truncate.

You mean the new order will be:
1) lock new_lock
2) start trans
3) lock osd_object::oo_sem.

Right?
Uploaded patch set 3.

Patch Set 4:

I found another reason for the different lock/trans order on the OST side. The write operation will take read lock in prep_write(), then start transaction in commit_wwrite(), and then read unlock. Such read lock will prevent others to truncate or destroy the target object before commit_write() done. If we adjust the order, then how to handle prep_write() and commit_write()?

Patch Set 4:

Alex, what's your idea for my former concern? As you can see, there is deadlock in the set 4 implementation, because of the read_lock in the prep_write() and commit_write().

parents.subject: LU-9183 ptlrpc: handle posix_acl: xattr representation cleanups

commit.subject: LU-8806 ofd: dt_lock object after starting transaction
commit.message:
LU-8806 ofd: dt_lock object after starting transaction

For ldiskfs backend, the punch transaction may be too large to
be held in one transaction, so the transaction maybe restarted
inside ldiskfs. If we geneately lock the target to be truncated
after starting the transaction, then restart the transaction may
cause deadlock with other modifications against the same object.

To avoid such deadlock, and also make OST side transaction/dt_lock
order to be the same as MDT side, we introduce some new mechanism:

ofd_object::ofo_punch_rwsem
It is OFD internal read/write semaphore, only OFD read/write/punch
handler will touch ofd_object::ofo_punch_rwsem: the punch handler
will take write mode semaphore before the punch transaction start;
IO handler will take read mode semaphore during prepare phase and
hold it until commit phase done, that prevents the punch handler
to truncate the target during the IO process.

osd_object::oo_trans_sem
It is OSD internal read/write semaphore for OST side modification:
if some OST side modification can be triggered with concurrent
punch operation, then it needs to take read mode semaphore during
declare phase. The punch handler will take write mode semaphore
during punch decalre. Then even though the punch transaction is
restarted inside ldiskfs, no others (modification) will wait for
the dt_lock on the object being truncated but with the transaction
started already.

With these changes, both MDT side and OST side can follow the
same rule: dt_lock object after starting transaction.

Signed-off-by: Fan Yong <fan.yong@intel.com>
Change-Id: Ie1e92f714063043c5d2285bdf25017fdab699f85
